name: Kabyle

allophone_rules.notes: Blocks spirantization of the lax dental /t/ after a homorganic nasal: the cluster ⟨nt⟩ surfaces [nt], not *[nθ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997, Berber Phonology, p.468: spirantization is blocked after a homorganic nasal). First-match ordering: this identity rewrite precedes KAB_SPIRANT_T, so a /t/ preceded by /n/ keeps its stop and never reaches the spirantization rule.
Blocks spirantization of the lax dental /d/ after a homorganic nasal: ⟨nd⟩ surfaces [nd], not *[nð] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468). Precedes KAB_SPIRANT_D. E.g. anda 'where' → [anda], not [anða].
Blocks spirantization of the lax emphatic dental /dˤ/ after a homorganic nasal (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468). Precedes KAB_SPIRANT_DH_EMPH.

Blocks spirantization of the lax bilabial /b/ after a homorganic nasal: written ⟨nb⟩ assimilates to [mb] (KAB_N_LABIAL_ASSIM), and the /b/ stays a stop [mb], not *[mβ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468: spirantization blocked after a homorganic nasal). Both /m/ and the underlying /n/ trigger the block so the rule fires whether or not the nasal-place-assimilation rewrite has been read on the preceding slot. Precedes KAB_SPIRANT_B.

Spirantization of the lax (non-geminate) bilabial stop: /b/ → [β]. Kossmann & Stroomer (1997), Berber Phonology, p.466: "Spirantization implies the development of lax stops into fricatives, e.g., b becoming ß"; "It reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle." The tense (geminate) counterpart, written ⟨bb⟩ and mapped to /bː/, is a distinct phoneme and is NOT targeted by this rule (p.466: "Spirantization never affects tense consonants").
Spirantization of the lax dental stop: /d/ → [ð] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466, 468: Riffian/Kabyle lax d → ð). E.g. adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. Geminate ⟨dd⟩ = /dː/ stays a stop.
Spirantization of the lax voiceless dental stop: /t/ → [θ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466-468). Geminate ⟨tt⟩ = /tː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop, with palatal shift: /k/ → [ç] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.469: Riffian/Kabyle k → ʃ or ç; Karaoui et al. 2024 list [ç] in the Kabyle fricative inventory). Geminate ⟨kk⟩ = /kː/ stays a stop.

Spirantization of the lax velar stop, with palatal shift: /ɡ/ → [ʝ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.469: Riffian/Kabyle g → ʝ; Karaoui et al. 2024 list [ʝ] in the Kabyle fricative inventory). E.g. argaz 'man' → [arʝaz]. Geminate ⟨gg⟩ = /ɡː/ stays a stop.
Spirantization of the lax emphatic (pharyngealized) dental stop: /dˤ/ → [ðˤ] (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468: spirantized ḍ). Geminate ⟨ḍḍ⟩ = /dˤː/ stays a stop.
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [ŋ] before a dorsal (velar/uvular) consonant.
Nasal place assimilation: /n/ → [m] before a labial consonant.

notes: Kabyle (Taqbaylit), Northern Berber (Amazigh) language of Kabylia, north-eastern Algeria, ~5-7 million speakers (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997, Berber Phonology, p.461: 'Kabyle Berber (Taqbaylit) is spoken by approximately 7 million'). INPUT CONTRACT: this spec expects text in the standardised Berber LATIN alphabet (the tamaziɣt/INALCO orthography descending from Mammeri's conventions, used by the Kabyle Wikipedia, the press, and Naït-Zerrad's teaching grammars). Tifinagh (the neo-Tifinagh abjad-alphabet) is a secondary script for Kabyle and is NOT modelled here; a Tifinagh text must be transliterated to the Latin orthography first. Hallmark grapheme->IPA correspondences: c=/ʃ/, č=/t͡ʃ/, ǧ=/d͡ʒ/, j=/ʒ/, ɣ=/ɣ/ (uvular [ʁ] realisation), x=/χ/, ḥ=/ħ/, ɛ=/ʕ/, q=/q/, and the emphatic (pharyngealized) series ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ = /rˤ sˤ dˤ tˤ zˤ/ (Proto-Berber had only ḍ and ẓ pharyngealized; the rest are Arabic-integrated — Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464). Three phonemic vowels /a i u/ with wide context-dependent realisation (a:[a~ɑ~æ], i:[e~i], u:[o~u]; Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.463); ⟨e⟩ writes the epenthetic schwa [ə], which is largely predictable from syllable structure / sonority and mostly non-phonemic (ibid. p.463 §23.4.2). THE SIGNATURE FEATURE is SPIRANTIZATION (modelled as allophone_rules): the lax (non-geminate) stops b d t k g ḍ surface as the fricatives [β ð θ ç ʝ ðˤ], while the tense (geminate) stops — written by doubling the letter (bb dd tt kk gg ḍḍ) and mapped to the distinct long phonemes /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/ — remain stops and are never spirantized (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.466: 'Spirantization never affects tense consonants'; the lax:tense opposition is durational — lax consonants are 'always realized shorter than their tense counterparts', p.465). Because the geminates are represented as separate long phonemes, the spirantization rules are naturally gemination-conditioned: they target /b d t k ɡ dˤ/ but not /bː dː tː kː ɡː dˤː/. e.g. argaz 'man' → [arʝaz], adrar 'mountain' → [aðrar]. SPIRANTIZATION IS BLOCKED after a homorganic nasal (clusters nt, nd, and nb→[mb]; Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.468): this is MODELLED by the KAB_POSTNASAL_HARD_* identity rules, which precede the KAB_SPIRANT_* rules so a lax stop after /n m/ keeps its stop (anda 'where' → [anda] not *[anða]; ⟨nb⟩ → [mb] not *[mβ]). First-match rule ordering supplies the negative context the rule layer cannot state directly. KNOWN LIMITS (documented, not modelled): (1) spirantization is likewise blocked after /l/ and in some tautosyllabic obstruent clusters in the fuller descriptions, but the primary source relied on here (Kossmann & Stroomer 1997) states only the homorganic-nasal block, so only that is modelled; a lax stop after /l/ may still be over-spirantized; (2) morphophonological gemination alternations (geminated ɣ surfaces as qq, geminated w as bb, geminated y as gg) are lexical/morphological and are not derivable from the Latin spelling; (3) Kabyle stress is largely non-distinctive and predictable, and is not marked here. Labiovelarized kʷ gʷ are usually left unwritten in the standard orthography and are not modelled.

ancestors.notes: Immediate Glottolog subgroup (kaby1244): Afro-Asiatic > Berber > Kabyle-Atlas Berber > Kabyle. Traditionally within Northern Berber.
Arabic adstrate/superstrate: heavy lexical borrowing and the source of most of Kabyle's non-Proto-Berber emphatics and pharyngeals (ḥ /ħ/, ɛ /ʕ/, ṣ /sˤ/, ṭ /tˤ/), per Kossmann & Stroomer 1997 p.464.

sources.author: Kossmann, Maarten G. & Stroomer, Harry J.
sources.title: Berber Phonology
sources.publisher: In A. S. Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa, vol. 1, Eisenbrauns
sources.notes: PRIMARY source, read in full. p.461: Kabyle ~7 million speakers. p.463 §23.4.1: three vowels a i u and their context realisations; §23.4.2 schwa placement by structure/sonority. p.464 §23.4.3.1: pharyngealization, Proto-Berber only ḍ ẓ pharyngealized. p.465 §23.4.3.1.2: lax vs tense opposition, lax always shorter than tense (geminate). p.466 §23.5.1: 'Spirantization implies the development of lax stops into fricatives, e.g., b becoming ß ... It reaches its culminating points in Riffian and Kabyle ... Spirantization never affects tense consonants.' p.468-469 §23.7.1: spirantized values b→ß, d→ð, g→ʝ/ɣ, k→ʃ/ç; spirantization blocked after a homorganic nasal.

sources.author: Karaoui, Fazia; Djeradi, Amar; Djeradi, Rachida
sources.title: Acoustic Characterization of the Noise Sources for the Kabyle Fricatives Consonants
sources.notes: Read. Confirms the Kabyle fricative inventory [s, f, ʃ, v, ʒ, z, ħ, h, ʕ, ç, ʝ] (p.123 abstract) — the spirantized velars [ç ʝ] are phonemic surface fricatives — and lists spirantization, labio-velarization, palatalization and affrication as Kabyle-specific features (p.123 §1).

sources.author: Naït-Zerrad, Kamal
sources.title: Grammaire moderne du kabyle / Tajerrumt tatrart n teqbaylit
sources.notes: SECONDARY reference (not read in full). The standard modern Kabyle reference grammar in the tamaziɣt/INALCO Latin orthography; used in the TTS gold set for lexical and morphological attestation of the authored sentences (verb conjugation, the état d'annexion, clitic order) — never as the source of a phonological reflex, which is grounded in kossmann_stroomer1997.

sources.author: Dallet, Jean-Marie
sources.title: Dictionnaire kabyle-français: parler des At Mangellat, Algérie
sources.notes: SECONDARY reference (not read in full). The standard Kabyle-French dictionary (At Mangellat / Aït Menguellet variety); used in the TTS gold set for lexical attestation of the authored sentences (headword spelling and gloss). Not used as the source of any phonological claim.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Kabyle language
sources.notes: SECONDARY cross-check (flagged). Used only to confirm the standard 34-letter Berber Latin alphabet grapheme table (c=ʃ, č=t͡ʃ, ǧ=d͡ʒ, j=ʒ, ɣ, x=χ, ḥ=ħ, ɛ=ʕ, q, emphatics ṛ ṣ ḍ ṭ ẓ, ⟨e⟩=schwa, spirantized b d g k t → β ð ʝ ç θ) against the primary phonology sources. NOT used as the source of any phonological claim.
